literal: the doctrine of 'in-some-respect'; from syāt ("in some respect; conditionally") + vāda ("doctrine")
invariant_definition: The Jaina seven-fold conditional predication-schema (*sapta-bhaṅgī*), the formal apparatus of *anekānta-vāda*. Each *bhaṅga* qualifies its predication with the indeclinable *syāt* ("in some respect"), marking the standpoint-conditioned character of the assertion. The seven *bhaṅgas* are: (1) *syād asti* — 'in some respect, [it] exists'; (2) *syād nāsti* — 'in some respect, [it] does not exist'; (3) *syād asti nāsti* — 'in some respect, [it] exists; in some respect, [it] does not exist' (the two predications conjoined under their respective standpoints, not jointly under the same standpoint); (4) *syād avaktavyaḥ* — 'in some respect, [it] is inexpressible' (when both predicates are required simultaneously under the same standpoint, the predication is *avaktavya* rather than contradictory); (5) *syād asty avaktavyaḥ* — 'in some respect, [it] exists; in some respect, [it] is inexpressible'; (6) *syād nāsty avaktavyaḥ* — 'in some respect, [it] does not exist; in some respect, [it] is inexpressible'; (7) *syād asti nāsty avaktavyaḥ* — the full combined predication. The schema is not relativism: each *bhaṅga* is a determinate predication conditioned by a determinate standpoint, and contradictory predicates are never asserted of the same subject under the same standpoint. The schema is closed (exactly seven *bhaṅgas*) and combinatorially complete with respect to the three primitive operations (assertion, denial, joint-assertion-as-inexpressible).

per_school.definition: The seven-fold *bhaṅga* predication is articulated in formal apparatus in Akalaṅka's *Aṣṭa-Sahasrī* and *Siddhi-Viniścaya* against Buddhist (Dharmakīrti-school) and Brahmanical opponents. The *avaktavya* (inexpressible) *bhaṅga* is the load-bearing innovation: when both *asti* and *nāsti* are required simultaneously under the same standpoint, the predication is *avaktavya* — neither both nor neither, but inexpressible-as-such — and this avoids contradiction.
Malliṣeṇa's *Syādvāda-Mañjarī* gives the standard thirteenth-century exposition of the seven-fold predication, with detailed attention to the standpoint-conditions and the avoidance of contradiction.

Yaśovijaya's *Jaina-Tarka-Bhāṣā* and *Nyāya-Loka* present the seven-fold *bhaṅga*-predication in Navya-Nyāya formal grammar, showing how each *bhaṅga* is conditioned by an explicit *avacchedaka*; the seven-fold scheme is integrated with classical *anumāna*-theory.

Madhusūdana's *Advaita-Siddhi* engages the seven-fold predication and argues that either the *bhaṅgas* are conditioned (in which case the doctrine is innocuous and no opponent denies it) or unconditioned (in which case it is straightforwardly self-contradictory); the *avaktavya* *bhaṅga* is read as a formal trick rather than a substantive metaphysical move.

translator_note: 'Conditional predication' or 'sevenfold predication' acceptable. The indeclinable *syāt* is NOT a modal operator — it does not mean 'possibly' in the modal-logical sense. It means 'in some respect' and marks the explicit standpoint-conditioning of the predication. Western readers should not assimilate *syād-vāda* to modal logic, fuzzy logic, or paraconsistent logic without careful qualification — the structural analogies exist but the metaphysical commitments are different.
